content.text: — each take an iterable of Promises and return a single new Promise that settles based on how the inputs settle. Choosing the right one determines both the correctness and the performance of parallel async work in React.
How All Four Behave — Visual Overview
Given three inputs — P1 resolves at t=1, P2 rejects at t=2, P3 resolves at t=3 — each combinator settles differently:

attrs.alt: Timeline diagram showing how Promise.all, allSettled, race, and any behave with three parallel promises where P1 resolves, P2 rejects, and P3 resolves
attrs.title: Promise combinator behaviour timeline

content.text: fulfills with an array of results when every input Promise fulfills. It rejects immediately (fail-fast) as soon as any input rejects — the remaining Promises are ignored (though they still run; you cannot cancel them).
waits for all input Promises to settle (in any order) and fulfills with an array of result objects — one per input. It
When to Use allSettled vs. all
All requests must succeed for the page to be useful
Show what succeeded; gracefully degrade on failures
Batch operations where partial success is acceptable
Need a summary of successes + failures for an audit log
resolves or rejects with the first Promise to settle — whether that is a fulfillment or a rejection. Remaining Promises are ignored (they still execute, but their results are discarded).
with an empty array returns a Promise that never settles — it stays pending forever. Always pass at least one input.
fulfills with the first Promise that fulfills, ignoring rejections. It only rejects — with an
— if every input Promise rejects. This makes it ideal for redundant parallel requests.
In React: Loading a Dashboard with Multiple APIs
A common React pattern is a dashboard that must load data from several endpoints. The choice of combinator directly impacts what the user experiences when one request fails:
In React: Fetch with Timeout Using Promise.race
Real applications often need more nuanced patterns than a single combinator provides. You can compose them:
Partial success OK; collect all outcomes
Forgetting that Promise.all Rejects on First Failure
rejects immediately, and the results of the other Promises are lost. Use
when you need all outcomes regardless of failures.
Promise.race With an Empty Array Hangs Forever
returns a Promise that never settles. Similarly,
with an empty array rejects with
when all Promises reject. This error wraps the individual rejection reasons in an
array. Check each one in error-reporting code:
Not Cancelling Ongoing Promises After Race Settles
settles, the other Promises still run to completion — they just have no handlers. For fetch calls, use
to cancel the remaining network requests and avoid wasting bandwidth:
Use this decision guide when you need multiple Promises to coordinate:
All must succeed or fail together
. Use for dependent operations like fetching a user and their posts when you need both.
Need all outcomes regardless of failures
. Use for dashboards, batch operations, or reporting where partial success is acceptable.
First to settle wins (success or failure)
. Use for timeouts (race a fetch against a timer) or cache-or-network patterns.
. Use when you have redundant sources (multiple CDNs, fallback APIs) and want whichever delivers first.
is fail-fast — one rejection rejects everything. Use it when all requests must succeed for the operation to be meaningful.
never rejects and gives you every outcome. Prefer it for dashboards and batch operations where partial success is acceptable.
settles with the first Promise to settle — fulfilled or rejected. Its primary use cases are timeouts and stale-while-revalidate patterns.
fulfills with the first fulfillment, skipping rejections. Use it for redundant parallel sources where you want the fastest success.
All four combinators fire all their input Promises — none can cancel in-flight work. Use
to cancel network requests explicitly when you no longer need the results.
to avoid state updates after unmount.
